name: Buddhist Mythology
description: One of the central virtues in Buddhism, the wish for all beings to be free from suffering. Combined with wisdom, compassion is the foundation of the bodhisattva path and is personified by Avalokiteshvara.
definition: Karuna - the Buddhist virtue of wishing all beings freedom from suffering, paired with wisdom (prajna) as the fundamental basis for enlightenment and ethical conduct.
examples: Compassionate witnessing of suffering without turning away
Extending loving-kindness equally to enemies and loved ones
Working to relieve hunger, disease, and ignorance

keyMyths.title: The Story of the Hare in the Moon
keyMyths.description: A hare sacrifices itself to feed a starving traveler, who is revealed to be Indra in disguise. Impressed by the hare's selfless compassion, Indra draws the hare's image on the moon for all to see, serving as a constant reminder of the virtue of compassion.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of Avalokiteshvara's Thousand Arms
keyMyths.description: Avalokiteshvara, overwhelmed by the suffering of the world, attempts to use his powers to help everyone, but his head splits into eleven pieces. Amitabha Buddha restores him and gives him a thousand arms and eyes, each representing his boundless compassion and ability to perceive and alleviate suffering.

keyMyths.title: The Story of Angulimala
keyMyths.description: Angulimala, a ruthless murderer, is converted by the Buddha, who demonstrates immense compassion and skillful means to guide him toward enlightenment. This story highlights the transformative power of compassion even for the most hardened individuals.

keyMyths.title: The Buddha's Previous Lives of Self-Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: In numerous Jataka tales, the Buddha, in his previous lives, demonstrates extraordinary acts of compassion, such as offering his own flesh to feed a starving tigress and her cubs, or giving away his kingdom and possessions to those in need. These exemplify the ultimate expression of compassion leading to Buddhahood.

keyMyths.title: The Bodhisattva Vow
keyMyths.description: Bodhisattvas take a vow to postpone their own final nirvana until all sentient beings are liberated from suffering. This vow embodies the essence of compassion, prioritizing the well-being of others above one's own enlightenment.

sources.name: The Jataka Tales
sources.description: A collection of stories about the previous lives of the Buddha, often illustrating virtues like compassion and wisdom.
sources.title: The Jataka Tales

sources.name: Karandavyuha Sutra
sources.description: A Mahayana sutra dedicated to Avalokiteshvara, the bodhisattva of compassion, and detailing his origins and powers.
sources.title: Karandavyuha Sutra

sources.name: Angulimala Sutta
sources.description: A Theravada Buddhist scripture that tells the story of Angulimala's conversion by the Buddha.
sources.title: Angulimala Sutta

sources.name: Bodhicaryavatara by Shantideva
sources.description: A Mahayana Buddhist text that provides a comprehensive guide to the Bodhisattva path, emphasizing the importance of compassion and altruism.
sources.title: Bodhicaryavatara by Shantideva

sources.name: The Heart Sutra
sources.description: A short but profound Mahayana sutra that emphasizes the emptiness of inherent existence and the importance of compassion in realizing enlightenment.
sources.title: The Heart Sutra
